progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: This paper presents a novel and potentially important side-channel attack on neural network confidentiality, with a compelling premise, clear motivation, and an engaging exposition that makes the physical intuition and security implications accessible. The introduction is well written and the threat model is mostly explicit, and the claimed ability to recover detailed layer structure from a cheap magnetic sensor is sufficiently surprising to be of broad interest to both ML security and systems audiences. That said, the paper appears to make very strong claims—e.g., reconstructing the “complete” structure of large and deep networks—that will require unusually careful experimental support, and the beginning raises several concerns about generality and realism. The attack assumes close physical proximity to the GPU power cable, knowledge of input dimension and batch size, the ability to issue a query, matching GPU hardware, and use of the same deep learning framework; these assumptions should be justified more rigorously and their impact on practical deployability should be quantified. The method also seems heavily dependent on trained classifiers and framework/hardware-specific execution signatures, so the paper should clearly separate what is learned about the neural architecture from what is inferred from implementation artifacts, and should test robustness across GPUs, drivers, frameworks, model compilation settings, background workloads, sensor placement, cable routing, and noisy environments. The reconstruction pipeline involving per-layer classification plus integer programming sounds promising, but the introduction does not yet clarify failure modes, ambiguity between architecturally equivalent implementations, or how similarity/recovery accuracy is measured for complex architectures with skip connections and fused operations. Overall, the work is intriguing and potentially high impact, but its quality will depend on whether the empirical evaluation convincingly supports the breadth of the claims and whether the authors temper overstatements, articulate limitations, and demonstrate robustness beyond a tightly controlled laboratory setting.

comments.title: “Passive observer” wording conflicts with the stated ability to launch a query
comments.quote: We examine the fluctuation of magnetic flux from the GPU’s power cable, and ask whether a passive observer can glean the information needed to reconstruct neural network structure.
comments.explanation: The attack is passive with respect to the victim machine internals—no malware, no code execution, and only magnetic observation—but the stated threat model also assumes the adversary can launch or control a query of known batch size. As written, “passive observer” could be misunderstood as requiring only observation of arbitrary victim traffic. The paper should clarify that the adversary is passive in measurement but active in triggering or controlling the inference query.

comments.title: The claim of reconstructing the “complete” network structure is stronger than the stated evidence and assumptions
comments.quote: Remarkably, we show that, through magnetic induction sensing, a passive observer can reconstruct the _complete_ network structure even for _large_ and _deep_ networks.
comments.explanation: The paper appears to recover architecture-level information—topology and hyperparameters—not weights, training data, or the learned function. The method also depends on substantial assumptions, including known input dimension and batch size, matching GPU/framework conditions, recognizable operation signatures, and supported hyperparameter classes. “Complete network structure” could therefore be read too broadly. A more qualified claim, such as complete structure for the evaluated architectures under the stated threat model, would better match the evidence.

comments.title: Electromagnetic-field physics statements are technically imprecise
comments.quote:
Current induces magnetic flux governed by the Biot-Savart law (Griffiths, 2005), and current fluctuations induce EM ripples whose propagation through the environment is governed by the Amp`ere-Maxwell law.

Because magnetic flux decays inversely proportional to the squared distance from the source, according to the Biot-Savart law (Griffiths, 2005), we position the sensor within millimeters of the cable casing (see Fig. 2).
comments.explanation: The practical intuition is right: GPU current fluctuations produce magnetic-field variations, and placing the sensor close to the cable improves signal strength. However, EM propagation is not governed by the Ampère-Maxwell law alone; it follows from Maxwell’s equations jointly, especially together with Faraday’s law and boundary/constitutive conditions. Also, Biot-Savart gives an inverse-square contribution for an infinitesimal current element, but the field near a long straight wire scales approximately as inverse distance after integration, with real cable geometry and return paths adding further complications. These statements should be phrased as qualitative intuition or corrected.

comments.title: Prior side-channel work is overgeneralized as requiring host malware
comments.quote: These methods placed a malware process on the machine hosting the black-box model. Our threat model does not involve introducing processes on the host.
comments.explanation: The contrast is valid for many cache and memory-access side-channel attacks that require co-residency or code execution on the victim host. But the sentence appears to cover all cited prior methods, including power-consumption and timing approaches, some of which may rely on external measurements or query observation rather than host malware. The comparison should be limited to the relevant subset or qualified to avoid overstating the novelty of avoiding host-side code execution.

comments.title: The GPU step/kernel abstraction is stated too strongly
comments.quote:
While there may be data dependencies between steps, there are no such dependencies within a step.

Transitions between steps, however, are synchronized ... This cross-step synchronization allows for implementations structured into modules, or GPU _kernels_.
comments.explanation: The high-level intuition is useful: many neural-network operations expose substantial parallelism and may appear as separable GPU kernels in common eager-mode framework executions. But the literal claims are too strong. Convolutions, matrix multiplications, pooling, and normalization can contain intra-step reductions or accumulation dependencies, even though they are parallelized. Conversely, modern DL execution can fuse operations, launch multiple kernels for one high-level operation, use asynchronous streams, or change kernels depending on cuDNN/runtime choices. The paper should state its execution-mode assumptions more explicitly, such as no aggressive graph/kernel fusion and comparable framework/library versions.

comments.title: Signal dependence on input values and batch size is stated too categorically
comments.quote:
Although the specific input data values do not influence the signal, the input data size does.

As the batch size increases, more GPU cores will be utilized in each step. The GPU consequently draws more power, which in turn strengthens the signal. Once all GPU cores are involved, further increase of input batch size will not increase the signal strength, but elongate the execution time until the GPU runs out of memory.
comments.explanation: For dense GPU inference, operation type and tensor size may dominate the magnetic signal, so the approximation is plausible in the evaluated setting. Still, the wording is too categorical. Numeric values can affect CMOS switching activity, memory behavior, sparsity, branch predicates, exceptional floating-point behavior, or data-dependent control flow. Likewise, batch size can change kernel selection, tiling, cache behavior, occupancy, memory bandwidth pressure, clocks, thermals, and fusion decisions, so signal strength need not follow a simple monotone-then-saturated pattern. These should be framed as empirical observations for the tested models/frameworks rather than general physical claims.

comments.title: Convolution hyperparameter constraints are not generally valid as stated
comments.quote:
Of the six CNN hyperparameters (stride, padding, dilation, input, output, and kernel size), any one is determined by the other five.

- (iii) The output size of a CNN layer does not exceed its input size (due to convolution).
comments.explanation: Standard convolution imposes useful algebraic consistency constraints, but the paper states them too broadly. Because output size usually involves a floor operation, fixing five of stride, padding, dilation, input size, output size, and kernel size does not always uniquely determine the sixth unless extra divisibility or exact-equality assumptions are imposed. Also, ordinary padded convolutions can produce output spatial sizes larger than the input, and transposed or upsampling convolutions can increase resolution as well. If the method intentionally restricts the search space to conventional image-classification CNNs, this should be described as a dataset/search-space heuristic rather than a general property of convolution.

comments.title: Sequential consistency constraints do not explain residual or branched architectures
comments.quote: - (i) The output size of a layer agrees with the input size of the next next layer.
comments.explanation: This constraint is natural for purely sequential networks, but the paper also claims to handle ResNet-style architectures and recognizes operations such as addition. Residual and branched networks require nonlocal tensor-shape constraints: skip outputs may be consumed several layers later, add operands must have compatible shapes, projection shortcuts and downsampling must be handled, and branch boundaries must be represented. The listed constraints only describe a chain of adjacent layers, leaving the integer-program encoding for residual dataflow underspecified.

comments.title: Notation around \(\alpha\) appears inconsistent with the stated parameter definitions
comments.quote: Constraint **(iv)** , however, is troublesome, because the appearance of kernel size _α_ and dilation _γ_ , both of which are optimization variables, make the constraint nonlinear.
comments.explanation: The surrounding text defines \(\alpha\) as stride and \(k\) as kernel size, and later text saying \(\alpha\) is often 1 or 2 also indicates that \(\alpha\) means stride. This is likely a local notation slip, but it occurs in the core optimization description and could confuse the source of nonlinearity. The text should say “stride \(\alpha\)” rather than “kernel size \(\alpha\).”

comments.title: Hyperparameter-estimation features are underspecified for reproduction
comments.quote: Each segment’s feature vector is assembled by **(i)** partitioning the segment uniformly into _N_ windows, and computing the average value of each window, **(ii)** concatenating the time duration of the segment. The concatenated feature vector has a length of 2 _N_ + 2.
comments.explanation: The feature design is plausible, but key choices are not specified in this passage: the value of \(N\), normalization and filtering, treatment of very short segments, and how the “immediately subsequent step” is represented when a linear layer is last or followed by an operation outside the listed set. These details can materially affect the recovery DNN’s input distribution and performance, so the procedure is difficult to reproduce unless they are specified elsewhere.

comments.title: Ease of solving the integer quadratic program is asserted without empirical support
comments.quote: The IP problem with a quadratic objective function and linear constraints can be easily solved, even when the number of hyperparameters is large (e.g., _>_ 1000).
comments.explanation: Using CPLEX for a quadratic objective with linear constraints is reasonable, and the authors acknowledge that integer programming is NP-complete in general. But the claim that such instances are “easily solved” for more than 1000 hyperparameters depends on variable bounds, integrality domains, constraint structure, solver settings, and tolerances. The paper should provide runtime statistics, search-space bounds, or a narrower empirical claim.

comments.title: Ambiguity in what the 64 test networks contain
comments.quote: Our test dataset consists of 64 randomly generated networks in a way similar to Sec. 4.2. The number of layers ranges from 30 to 50 layers. To diversify our zoology of test models, we also include smaller networks that are under 10 layers, LSTM networks, as well as ResNets (18, 34, 50, and 101).
comments.explanation: The passage first suggests that the test set consists of 64 randomly generated networks with 30–50 layers, but then says smaller networks, LSTMs, and named ResNets are also included. It is unclear whether the 64 networks include both random and curated cases or whether the curated cases are additional. This matters because reported average reconstruction metrics depend on the exact test-set mixture.

comments.title: Per-sample classification results are described as step-level correctness
comments.quote: As discussed in Sec. 3, we use a biLSTM model to predict the network step for each single sample. Table 1 reports its accuracy, measured on an Nvidia TITAN V GPU.
comments.explanation: The model predicts a label for each signal sample, and the table appears to report sample-level performance. But the surrounding wording frames the result as “classification accuracy of network steps” and later suggests that most network steps are classified correctly. This distinction matters because long operations such as convolutions contribute many more samples than short operations such as pooling or activations. High sample-weighted accuracy does not necessarily imply high step-level or segmentation accuracy.

comments.title: Reported hyperparameter “accuracy” is underspecified for regression outputs
comments.quote: Next, we report the test accuracies of our DNN models (discussed in Sec. 3.2) for estimating hyperparameters of convolutional layers. Our test data here consists of 1804 convolutional layers. On average, our DNN models have **96** _∼_ **97%** accuracy.
comments.explanation: Earlier, some hyperparameters are estimated by regression with mean-squared error, while others are classified. The passage does not define what “accuracy” means for regression outputs: exact integer match after rounding, tolerance-based match, relative error threshold, or consistency after the integer program would imply different interpretations. The 96–97% claim is therefore hard to assess without a metric definition.

comments.title: Downstream task-accuracy evaluation is overinterpreted and not fully aligned with the threat model
comments.quote:
Ultimately, the reconstruction quality must be evaluated by how well the reconstructed network performs in the task that the original network aims for.

Both the reconstructed and original networks are trained with the same training dataset for the same number of epochs.

The results in Table 2 show that for all seven networks, including large networks (e.g., ResNet101), the reconstructed networks perform almost as well as their original versions.
comments.explanation: Task accuracy after retraining is a useful downstream utility measure, but it is not the ultimate or direct measure of structural reconstruction quality: different architectures can achieve similar accuracy, and a structurally correct architecture can underperform due to training variance. The evaluation also trains reconstructed networks on the same dataset as the original, although the threat model says the adversary lacks the victim’s training data and data distribution; this should be framed as an oracle-style evaluation rather than an attack capability. Finally, the claim that all reconstructed networks perform “almost as well” should be qualified if some table entries show drops of several percentage points.

comments.title: Transfer-attack results overstate the “white-box” interpretation and comparability
comments.quote: Using our side-channel-based surrogate model, the transfer attack success rate is comparable to the _white-box_ transfer attack baseline, that is, using the target model’s network structure (but trained separately) to attack the target model itself. In other words, our sidechannel-based reconstruction effectively turn a black-box attack into a white-box attack.
comments.explanation: Knowing the target architecture but training separate weights is not normally a white-box attack in adversarial ML; white-box access usually means access to the actual target parameters and gradients. The evidence supports the claim that architecture recovery can produce a strong transfer surrogate, not that the black-box setting becomes truly white-box. In addition, the table appears to show variability across architectures and GPUs, with some gaps between reconstructed surrogates and same-architecture baselines that are not negligible. The statement should use standard transfer-attack terminology and report averages/ranges or note exceptions.

comments.title: Defense discussion is underspecified and sometimes overbroad
comments.quote:
Since our approach is passive in that it does not alter any code or hardware operation of GPUs, detection methods which consist of somehow discovering someone is listening to the GPU are not applicable to magnetic leakage.

To prevent this behavior, one can keep the input sufficiently small (e.g. 1 single image) such that the magnetic signals never reach any stable state and suffer from a low signal-to-noise ratio, rendering our sensing setup futile.

Specifically, during the inference of a large input batch, we ran a third-party CUDA GPU stress test in the background ... We found that the magnetic signals are completely distorted ... Moreover, we observe little speed degradation for the foreground inference.
comments.explanation: The proposed defenses are plausible, but the claims are stronger than the supporting detail. Software detection of altered execution may not apply, but physical/tamper detection, enclosure monitoring, access control, shielding, or environmental EM monitoring could still be relevant. Restricting batch size may defeat the demonstrated setup, but better sensors, repeated measurements, averaging, or query control could reduce that protection. The jamming/stress-test defense is also underspecified: its effectiveness and performance overhead depend on GPU model, kernel mix, occupancy, memory bandwidth, scheduling, batch size, and throttling. The paper should provide parameters, slowdown numbers, and attack-degradation metrics, and frame these as mitigations rather than categorical prevention.

progressive_original__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive Original (gpt-5.5)
progressive_original__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: This paper presents a novel and potentially important side-channel attack on neural network confidentiality, with a compelling premise, clear motivation, and an engaging exposition that makes the physical intuition and security implications accessible. The introduction is well written and the threat model is mostly explicit, and the claimed ability to recover detailed layer structure from a cheap magnetic sensor is sufficiently surprising to be of broad interest to both ML security and systems audiences. That said, the paper appears to make very strong claims—e.g., reconstructing the “complete” structure of large and deep networks—that will require unusually careful experimental support, and the beginning raises several concerns about generality and realism. The attack assumes close physical proximity to the GPU power cable, knowledge of input dimension and batch size, the ability to issue a query, matching GPU hardware, and use of the same deep learning framework; these assumptions should be justified more rigorously and their impact on practical deployability should be quantified. The method also seems heavily dependent on trained classifiers and framework/hardware-specific execution signatures, so the paper should clearly separate what is learned about the neural architecture from what is inferred from implementation artifacts, and should test robustness across GPUs, drivers, frameworks, model compilation settings, background workloads, sensor placement, cable routing, and noisy environments. The reconstruction pipeline involving per-layer classification plus integer programming sounds promising, but the introduction does not yet clarify failure modes, ambiguity between architecturally equivalent implementations, or how similarity/recovery accuracy is measured for complex architectures with skip connections and fused operations. Overall, the work is intriguing and potentially high impact, but its quality will depend on whether the empirical evaluation convincingly supports the breadth of the claims and whether the authors temper overstatements, articulate limitations, and demonstrate robustness beyond a tightly controlled laboratory setting.

comments.title: “Passive observer” wording conflicts with the stated ability to launch a query
comments.quote: We examine the fluctuation of magnetic flux from the GPU’s power cable, and ask whether a passive observer can glean the information needed to reconstruct neural network structure.
comments.explanation: At first this reads as a purely passive eavesdropping attack: the adversary only observes an existing computation. I checked the immediately following threat model and the abstract, however, and the attack is not purely passive in that sense: the adversary is assumed to be able to launch a query of known batch size. The authors may intend “passive” to mean passive with respect to the victim machine internals—no malware, no access to binaries, no code execution, and only magnetic observation of the cable—which is an important and valid distinction. What remains potentially misleading is that the reconstruction setup relies on actively causing or at least controlling a query event, especially because batch size is part of the assumed knowledge. A careful reader could therefore misunderstand the threat model as requiring only observation of arbitrary victim traffic.

comments.title: The claim of reconstructing the “complete” network structure is stronger than the stated evidence and assumptions
comments.quote: Remarkably, we show that, through magnetic induction sensing, a passive observer can reconstruct the _complete_ network structure even for _large_ and _deep_ networks.
comments.explanation: The surrounding text makes clear that the target is architecture-level information—topology and hyperparameters—not weights, training data, or learned function values. Later context also explains substantial assumptions: known input dimension and batch size, matching GPU brand/version, same deep learning framework, pretrained recovery models on similar hardware, and a supported set of layer/kernel signatures. Under that intended meaning, the sentence is directionally consistent with the paper’s goal. Still, “complete network structure” is a broad phrase and could be read as guaranteeing recovery of all architectural details for arbitrary large/deep networks. In practice, the method appears empirical and constrained by recognizable operations, framework/kernel behavior, and the hyperparameter classes modeled by the recovery DNNs and integer program. A more qualified phrasing such as “complete structure for the evaluated architectures under our threat model” would avoid overstatement.

comments.title: Electromagnetic propagation is attributed too narrowly to the Ampère-Maxwell law
comments.quote: Current induces magnetic flux governed by the Biot-Savart law (Griffiths, 2005), and current fluctuations induce EM ripples whose propagation through the environment is governed by the Amp`ere-Maxwell law.
comments.explanation: The physical intuition is broadly right: changing GPU current produces magnetic-field variations that can be sensed near the power cable, and invoking Maxwellian electromagnetism is appropriate. I also recognize this is introductory prose rather than a derivation. However, electromagnetic wave/ripple propagation is not governed by the Ampère-Maxwell law alone; in standard electromagnetism it follows from Maxwell’s equations jointly, especially Ampère-Maxwell together with Faraday’s law, plus constitutive/boundary conditions. Biot-Savart is also a quasi-static result, so its use alongside time-varying propagation language is an approximation. This does not undermine the attack, but the sentence is technically imprecise and could be made more accurate by saying the fields are described by Maxwell’s equations, with Biot-Savart giving the quasi-static magnetic-field intuition near the cable.

comments.title: Prior side-channel work is overgeneralized as requiring host malware
comments.quote: These methods placed a malware process on the machine hosting the black-box model. Our threat model does not involve introducing processes on the host.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended contrast: the authors want to distinguish their proximity-only magnetic probe from cache/memory/timing attacks that require co-residency or code execution on the victim host. That contrast is valid for many cache and memory-access side-channel papers, where an attacker process must share hardware resources with the victim. However, the sentence as written applies to all cited methods in the preceding list, including power-consumption-profile attacks. Some power side-channel model-extraction work relies on external measurement equipment or power sensors rather than a malware process on the host, and timing attacks can also be query-observation attacks rather than necessarily malware-based. If the authors mean only the cache/memory subset, or only the specific implementations in these citations, this should be qualified. As written, the comparison may overstate the novelty of avoiding host-side code execution.

comments.title: One-to-one mapping between network steps and GPU kernels is too strong
comments.quote: The parallel nature of GPU computation lends itself to a natural implementation of networks, wherein each step is executed in parallel, i.e., single instruction multiple data (SIMD) parallelism. Transitions between steps, however, are synchronized (Buck, 2007): in our example above, activation begins only after pooling completes. This cross-step synchronization allows for implementations structured into modules, or GPU _kernels_ . This modular approach is employed in widely-used deep learning frameworks such as PyTorch and TensorFlow (Paszke et al., 2019; Abadi et al., 2016).
comments.explanation: The authors' intuition is helpful, and in many standard eager-mode PyTorch/TensorFlow executions, layer-like operations do appear as separable kernel launches with observable gaps. I also see that later sections train on traces from the same framework/hardware, which partially mitigates the issue empirically. Still, the prose suggests a fairly general mapping from each computational step to a synchronized GPU kernel boundary. Modern DL execution often violates this: operations may be fused, a single high-level operation such as convolution may launch multiple kernels, libraries such as cuDNN may choose different algorithms, and GPU streams can queue kernels asynchronously without a full device-wide synchronization or substantial idle period between every operation. The attack may still work under the authors' experimental configuration, but the text should state the execution-mode assumptions more explicitly, e.g., no aggressive graph/kernel fusion, comparable library versions, and identifiable kernel boundaries. Otherwise a reader could overinfer that the method applies unchanged to optimized inference runtimes such as TensorRT/XLA or fused operator graphs.

comments.title: Claim that there are no dependencies within a step is technically inaccurate
comments.quote: While there may be data dependencies between steps, there are no such dependencies within a step.
comments.explanation: I take the intended meaning to be that a step is the unit of work whose outputs are consumed by later steps, and that many elementwise or dense operations expose enough parallelism for GPU execution. That is a reasonable high-level abstraction for explaining why step boundaries can be visible. However, the literal claim that there are no data dependencies within a step is not generally true. Pooling has reductions over input windows, convolutions and matrix multiplications have accumulation dependencies for each output element, and normalization/reduction-style kernels may involve intra-kernel synchronization or staged reductions. These dependencies may not prevent parallel GPU execution, but they do exist algorithmically and can affect kernel structure and timing. A more accurate statement would be that steps are typically implemented with substantial intra-step parallelism, while inter-step dependencies impose ordering between high-level operations.

comments.title: Input values are said not to influence the signal too categorically
comments.quote: Although the specific input data values do not influence the signal, the input data size does.
comments.explanation: The surrounding context helps: the authors first say the signal correlates strongly with the kind of GPU operation rather than floating-point values, and they point to empirical verification in Sec. 5. For dense neural-network inference on GPUs, the instruction trace is often largely independent of the actual numeric values, so this is a plausible working approximation. The remaining concern is the categorical wording. CMOS switching activity, memory behavior, sparsity, exceptional floating-point values, branch predicates in some kernels, and data-dependent control flow can all make power/EM leakage value-dependent to some degree. Even if this dependence is small relative to operation-type signatures in the authors' experiments, saying values "do not influence" the signal is stronger than what the evidence can usually support. Since the threat model relies on unknown input values being irrelevant, this should be phrased as an empirical robustness claim for the tested dense models/frameworks rather than a general physical statement.

comments.title: Batch-size effect on GPU utilization and signal strength is oversimplified
comments.quote: As the batch size increases, more GPU cores will be utilized in each step. The GPU consequently draws more power, which in turn strengthens the signal. Once all GPU cores are involved, further increase of input batch size will not increase the signal strength, but elongate the execution time until the GPU runs out of memory.
comments.explanation: The qualitative point is reasonable: larger batches can improve occupancy and signal-to-noise by increasing work per kernel, and after saturation one may mainly see longer execution. However, the described relationship is too monotone and clean for GPU inference. Batch size can change cuDNN/kernel algorithm selection, tiling, memory-bandwidth pressure, cache behavior, occupancy, clock/thermal behavior, and even whether particular kernels are fused or selected. Power may continue to change after nominal core occupancy is high, or may decrease/increase non-monotonically depending on whether the workload is compute- or memory-bound. The later advice to choose a suitable batch size empirically on a matching GPU partially addresses this, but the physical explanation should be softened so readers do not assume a universal saturation threshold or unchanged signal signatures across batch sizes.

comments.title: Convolution hyperparameters are not always uniquely determined by the other five
comments.quote: Of the six CNN hyperparameters (stride, padding, dilation, input, output, and kernel size), any one is determined by the other five.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended point: standard convolutional layers impose a strong algebraic relationship among input size, output size, stride, padding, dilation, and kernel size, so these quantities can be used as consistency constraints during reconstruction. However, under the standard deep-learning convention, the spatial output size is typically \(\left\lfloor (i + 2p - d(k-1) - 1)/s + 1 \right\rfloor\). Because of the floor, the inverse relation is not generally one-to-one: fixing five quantities does not always uniquely determine the sixth, and in some directions it may only constrain the missing value to an interval or require additional divisibility assumptions. The statement would be correct only if the authors restrict to parameter combinations satisfying an exact no-floor equality, or if they define their generated/search space so that the formula is invertible. That restriction is not stated here, and it matters because the later integer program appears to rely on these equalities as hard consistency constraints.

comments.title: Sequential consistency constraints do not specify how residual or branched architectures are handled
comments.quote: - (i) The output size of a layer agrees with the input size of the next next layer.
comments.explanation: The basic idea is sound for purely sequential networks: adjacent layers must agree on tensor dimensions. I also noticed from the broader paper context that the topology classifier recognizes operations such as `add`, and the experiments claim recovery of ResNet-style architectures, so the authors likely intend to handle nontrivial dataflow. But the constraints listed here only describe a chain of consecutive layers. In residual networks, a layer output may be consumed several layers later by an addition, and the add operands must have compatible shapes; similarly, branch boundaries, projection shortcuts, concatenations if present, and skip-path downsampling introduce constraints that are not equivalent to simple adjacency. The passage does not explain how the integer program encodes these nonlocal dependencies. This leaves an important part of the reconstruction method underspecified for one of the paper’s central claimed settings, namely ResNet-like models.

comments.title: The constraint that convolution output size cannot exceed input size is not generally valid
comments.quote: - (iii) The output size of a CNN layer does not exceed its input size (due to convolution).
comments.explanation: I see why this heuristic may work for many conventional image-classification CNNs, where spatial resolution is usually preserved or reduced by convolution/pooling. But as stated, it is not a general property of convolution. With sufficient zero padding, a standard convolution can produce a spatial output larger than the input; transposed convolutions or other convolution-like layers can also increase resolution. Even within ordinary convolutions, the standard formula permits \(o > i\) for some valid choices of padding, stride, dilation, and kernel size. If the authors’ training/test distribution excludes such layers, then this should be framed as a dataset/search-space restriction or heuristic, not as something true “due to convolution.” Otherwise the integer program may incorrectly rule out valid victim architectures.

comments.title: Notation around \(\alpha\) appears inconsistent with the stated parameter definitions
comments.quote: Constraint **(iv)** , however, is troublesome, because the appearance of kernel size _α_ and dilation _γ_ , both of which are optimization variables, make the constraint nonlinear.
comments.explanation: The surrounding text defines \(\alpha\), \(\beta\), \(\gamma\), and \(k\) as stride, padding, dilation, and kernel size, respectively. The later statement that \(\alpha\) is often 1 or 2 also confirms that \(\alpha\) is meant to be stride, not kernel size. So this is probably a local notation slip rather than a conceptual error. Still, it is a real source of confusion in the core optimization description: the nonlinearity arises from products involving variable stride/output size and dilation/kernel size, and the proposed simplification fixes stride \(\alpha\) and dilation \(\gamma\). The text should say “stride \(\alpha\)” rather than “kernel size \(\alpha\)” to avoid contradicting its own definitions.

comments.title: Hyperparameter-estimation features are underspecified for reproduction
comments.quote: Each segment’s feature vector is assembled by **(i)** partitioning the segment uniformly into _N_ windows, and computing the average value of each window, **(ii)** concatenating the time duration of the segment. The concatenated feature vector has a length of 2 _N_ + 2.
comments.explanation: The feature design is plausible: windowed averages plus duration should capture both signal shape and execution time while producing a fixed-length input. However, the method depends on choices that are not specified here. The value of \(N\) is not given, nor are signal normalization/filtering choices, treatment of very short segments, or how the “immediately subsequent step” is represented when a linear layer is last or when the next operation is not one of the listed activation/pooling/batch-normalization steps. These choices can materially affect the recovery DNN’s input distribution and performance. Unless they are specified elsewhere, this leaves the hyperparameter-estimation procedure difficult to reproduce.

comments.title: Magnetic field decay from a power cable is misstated as inverse-square
comments.quote: Because magnetic flux decays inversely proportional to the squared distance from the source, according to the Biot-Savart law (Griffiths, 2005), we position the sensor within millimeters of the cable casing (see Fig. 2).
comments.explanation: The practical conclusion is right: placing the sensor close to the power cable improves signal strength. But the stated physical scaling is not generally correct for the relevant geometry. The Biot-Savart law gives an inverse-square dependence for the contribution of an infinitesimal current element, but after integrating along a long straight wire, the magnetic flux density scales approximately as \(B = \mu_0 I/(2\pi r)\), i.e., inverse distance, not inverse squared. Real GPU power cables have finite geometry and return-current paths, so the exact field can be more complicated, but the simple inverse-square statement is misleading. This does not undermine the attack demonstration, but it should be corrected or qualified.

comments.title: Ease of solving the integer quadratic program is asserted without empirical bounds
comments.quote: The IP problem with a quadratic objective function and linear constraints can be easily solved, even when the number of hyperparameters is large (e.g., _>_ 1000).
comments.explanation: The authors correctly acknowledge just above that integer programming is NP-complete in general, and using CPLEX for a convex quadratic objective with linear constraints is a reasonable practical choice. What remains somewhat overstated is the unqualified claim that the problem can be “easily solved” for more than 1000 hyperparameters. For mixed-integer quadratic programs, tractability depends heavily on variable bounds, constraint structure, integrality domains, and solver tolerances. A reader trying to assess scalability would need runtime statistics, bounds on candidate hyperparameter ranges, or details of the CPLEX configuration. This is likely fixable with empirical timing results or a narrower claim, but as written it is an unsupported methodological assertion.

comments.title: Ambiguity in what the 64 test networks contain
comments.quote: Our test dataset consists of 64 randomly generated networks in a way similar to Sec. 4.2. The number of layers ranges from 30 to 50 layers. To diversify our zoology of test models, we also include smaller networks that are under 10 layers, LSTM networks, as well as ResNets (18, 34, 50, and 101).
comments.explanation: I initially read this as saying the entire test set consists of 64 randomly generated networks, all with 30–50 layers. The next sentence then says the authors also include smaller networks under 10 layers, LSTMs, and named ResNets, which cannot all satisfy the preceding description. I checked the surrounding text: later the paper says “Among the 64 tested networks,” which suggests the 64 may include both the random networks and the curated/smaller/ResNet cases, rather than being 64 random networks plus additional curated networks. If that is the intent, the current wording is misleading. This matters because the reported average Levenshtein distance and exact-match count depend on the mixture of random versus curated architectures, and a reader cannot clearly tell the test-set composition from this passage alone.

comments.title: Per-sample classification results are described as step-level correctness
comments.quote: As discussed in Sec. 3, we use a biLSTM model to predict the network step for each single sample. Table 1 reports its accuracy, measured on an Nvidia TITAN V GPU.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly state that the biLSTM predicts a label for each signal sample, and Table 1’s last column is explicitly “# samples.” However, the surrounding prose and table caption frame the result as “Classification accuracy of network steps,” and later the discussion says that “the majority of network steps are classified correctly.” I checked whether this is merely a harmless shorthand, but the distinction is important here: convolutional steps last much longer and therefore contribute many more signal samples than lightweight steps such as pooling or activations. A high sample-weighted F1 or accuracy does not necessarily imply high step-level accuracy, nor does it directly imply accurate topology segmentation. The later Levenshtein-distance evaluation partially addresses topology quality, but Table 1 itself should be described more precisely as per-sample labeling performance, not step-level classification performance.

comments.title: Reported hyperparameter “accuracy” is underspecified for regression outputs
comments.quote: Next, we report the test accuracies of our DNN models (discussed in Sec. 3.2) for estimating hyperparameters of convolutional layers. Our test data here consists of 1804 convolutional layers. On average, our DNN models have **96** _∼_ **97%** accuracy.
comments.explanation: Earlier in Sec. 3.2, the method uses regression with mean-squared error for size-like hyperparameters and classification for small discrete hyperparameters. Given that setup, I looked for how “accuracy” is defined for the regression-predicted hyperparameters: exact integer match after rounding, within-one tolerance, relative error threshold, or consistency after integer programming would all be plausible but would mean different things. The passage does not specify this. The appendix table may contain the breakdown, but the main claim of 96–97% average hyperparameter accuracy is hard to interpret without the metric definition, especially because size parameters can range widely and small absolute errors may or may not affect the reconstructed architecture.

comments.title: Classification accuracy is a weak proxy for structural reconstruction quality
comments.quote: Ultimately, the reconstruction quality must be evaluated by how well the reconstructed network performs in the task that the original network aims for.
comments.explanation: I understand the authors’ intent: after topology and hyperparameter recovery, they want an end-to-end measure of whether the recovered architecture is useful. That is a reasonable additional experiment. However, the word “must” overstates the case. Task accuracy after retraining is only an indirect proxy for structural reconstruction quality: different architectures can achieve similar CIFAR-10 accuracy, while a structurally incorrect architecture could still perform well after training; conversely, a correctly recovered architecture could underperform because of optimization variance. The paper already uses more direct structure metrics such as Levenshtein distance and hyperparameter accuracy, so task accuracy is best framed as a downstream utility measure rather than the ultimate measure of reconstruction quality.

comments.title: Evaluation uses training data unavailable under the stated threat model
comments.quote: We then train those reconstructed networks and compare their test accuracies with the original networks’ performance. Both the reconstructed and original networks are trained with the same training dataset for the same number of epochs.
comments.explanation: The experiment is understandable as an offline evaluation of whether the recovered architecture can support a model with comparable task performance. Still, I checked it against the stated threat model, where the adversary lacks access to the victim’s training data and underlying data distribution. Training the reconstructed architecture on the same dataset as the original therefore is not an attack step available to the adversary as described. The passage would be clearer if it explicitly framed this as an oracle-style evaluation of architectural fidelity or downstream potential, not as something the attacker can necessarily perform. Otherwise readers may overinterpret the reported CIFAR-10 accuracies as achievable by the adversary in the threat model.

comments.title: “Almost as well” is not uniformly supported by the table values
comments.quote: The results in Table 2 show that for all seven networks, including large networks (e.g., ResNet101), the reconstructed networks perform almost as well as their original versions.
comments.explanation: The qualitative conclusion is plausible for several entries in Table 2, where reconstructed models are within a small number of percentage points of the target accuracy, and in some cases even higher. However, reading the table values, some reconstructed accuracies appear substantially below their targets—for example, entries around 85% for ResNet-style models whose targets are around 91%, and an entry around 81.68% depending on the row/column parsing of the OCR table. Even allowing for OCR formatting ambiguity, the table seems to contain cases with roughly 5–7 percentage-point drops, which is not obviously “almost as well” on CIFAR-10. The authors could either qualify the statement, report mean/maximum drops explicitly, or explain why these gaps are acceptable in the context of their evaluation.

comments.title: Architecture-only surrogate is described as a white-box attack
comments.quote: Using our side-channel-based surrogate model, the transfer attack success rate is comparable to the _white-box_ transfer attack baseline, that is, using the target model’s network structure (but trained separately) to attack the target model itself. In other words, our sidechannel-based reconstruction effectively turn a black-box attack into a white-box attack.
comments.explanation: I understand the authors’ intended comparison: the side-channel reconstruction is being evaluated against a strong surrogate baseline that knows the target architecture but not the target’s learned weights. That is a useful and relevant baseline for transferability, and Table 3 does appear to compare reconstructed architectures with separately trained source models of known architectures. However, calling this a “white-box” baseline is nonstandard and potentially misleading. In adversarial ML, a white-box attack normally means access to the actual target model, including its trained parameters and gradients; a separately trained model with the same architecture is still a surrogate/transfer setting. The sentence then goes further by saying the reconstruction “effectively turn[s] a black-box attack into a white-box attack,” but the attack still lacks the target weights, training data distribution may differ, and gradients are those of a surrogate. The evidence supports “architecture-recovery yields a strong transfer surrogate,” not that the adversary obtains a true white-box attack.

comments.title: Transfer-attack comparison may overstate comparability across all targets
comments.quote: Using our side-channel-based surrogate model, the transfer attack success rate is comparable to the _white-box_ transfer attack baseline
comments.explanation: I checked this against the numbers in Table 3, interpreting the top four GPU rows as side-channel reconstructed surrogates and the diagonal entries in the bottom source-model block as the same-architecture separately trained baseline. For several targets the claim is broadly supported: e.g., ResNet-18 and ResNet-34 side-channel rows are close to the same-architecture baselines, and AlexNet is in the same rough range. But the table also seems to contain cases where the gap is not negligible, such as VGG-16 on GTX-1080 at 55.19 versus the VGG-16 same-architecture baseline around 74.29, and ResNet-101 reconstructions sometimes around the mid-80s versus a same-architecture baseline around 92.95. These may still be impressive transfer results, but “comparable” without qualification obscures variability across GPUs and architectures. A more precise statement, such as reporting averages and ranges or noting exceptions, would better match the table.

comments.title: Jamming defense is underspecified and the performance claim is insufficiently supported
comments.quote: Specifically, during the inference of a large input batch, we ran a third-party CUDA GPU stress test in the background (Timonen, 2020). We found that the magnetic signals are completely distorted because of the constantly high utilization of GPU. Moreover, we observe little speed degradation for the foreground inference.
comments.explanation: The basic idea is plausible: concurrent GPU activity could mask operation-specific power fluctuations and make side-channel segmentation harder. The authors also appropriately mention higher power consumption as a caveat. What remains problematic is that the defense is not described in enough detail to assess or reproduce, and the “little speed degradation” claim is surprising without measurements. A CUDA stress test’s effect depends heavily on its kernel mix, occupancy, memory bandwidth usage, priority/scheduling behavior, GPU model, batch size, and whether the victim inference already saturates compute or memory. For a “large input batch,” one might expect a competing stress workload to contend for resources and slow inference unless it is carefully throttled or uses otherwise idle resources. The passage gives no slowdown numbers, stress-test parameters, or attack-degradation metric, so the defense result is currently more anecdotal than substantiated.

comments.title: Batch-size prevention claim is stated too broadly relative to the earlier threat model and sensor caveat
comments.quote: To prevent this behavior, one can keep the input sufficiently small (e.g. 1 single image) such that the magnetic signals never reach any stable state and suffer from a low signal-to-noise ratio, rendering our sensing setup futile.
comments.explanation: The authors’ intent is clear and consistent with the earlier observation that larger batches improve signal strength and produce more stable operation-level signatures. As a defense against the particular prototype, forcing single-image inference could indeed reduce SNR. However, the wording risks overgeneralizing. Earlier context says that a higher-sampling-rate/higher-SNR sensor could reduce the minimum batch size needed, and the threat model allows the adversary to launch a query of known batch size unless the service explicitly restricts batching. Thus this is better framed as a rate/batch-size limiting mitigation against the demonstrated setup, not a general prevention mechanism. The phrase “never reach any stable state” is also stronger than what has been established; some operations may still be identifiable at small batch sizes with better sensors, repeated measurements, or averaging if the service permits it.

comments.title: Detection is dismissed too categorically
comments.quote: Since our approach is passive in that it does not alter any code or hardware operation of GPUs, detection methods which consist of somehow discovering someone is listening to the GPU are not applicable to magnetic leakage.
comments.explanation: It is fair that software-level detection based on altered execution, malware, timing perturbations, or anomalous queries would not directly apply to a purely passive electromagnetic measurement. That is an important distinction from many prior side-channel attacks. Still, the statement as written is broader than justified. A sensor must be placed in close proximity to the GPU power cable under this threat model, so physical/tamper detection, enclosure monitoring, access control, cable shielding with intrusion detection, or environmental EM monitoring could in principle be relevant. The authors may only mean that runtime software detection is not applicable, but “detection methods” in general being “not applicable” is too categorical and could mislead readers about the defensive space.
